article_title: Open source package with 1 million monthly downloads stole user credentials
core_thesis: The open-source package 'element-data' (1M+ monthly downloads) was compromised via a GitHub Actions workflow vulnerability, allowing attackers to exfiltrate credentials and publish a malicious update. This incident highlights the critical risk of supply-chain attacks through insecure CI/CD pipelines and the need for rigorous security audits of developer workflows.
detailed_summary: On April 27, 2026, unknown attackers exploited a vulnerability in the GitHub Actions workflow of the open-source package 'element-data' (a CLI for monitoring ML systems) to gain access to signing keys and account tokens. They published a malicious version (0.23.3) to PyPI and Docker Hub, which, when executed, stole user profiles, cloud provider keys, API tokens, SSH keys, and other credentials. The malicious package was removed within 12 hours after a third-party report. The developers fixed the vulnerability, rotated credentials, and urged users to check for compromise by looking for a marker file (/tmp/.trinny-security-update on Linux/macOS, %TEMP%\.trinny-security-update on Windows). The attack vector involved posting malicious code to a pull request that triggered a bash script in the developer's account, retrieving sensitive data. This incident underscores the growing threat of supply-chain attacks on open-source repositories, especially through insecure user-developed workflows like GitHub Actions.

entities.name: element-data
entities.role: Compromised open-source package (CLI for ML monitoring) with 1M+ monthly downloads.

entities.name: GitHub Actions
entities.role: CI/CD platform whose workflow vulnerability was exploited to gain access to developer credentials.

entities.name: HD Moore
entities.role: Founder/CEO of runZero, commented on the prevalence of vulnerabilities in user-developed repository workflows.

entities.name: Dan Goodin
entities.role: Senior Security Editor at Ars Technica, author of the article.

key_data_points: The malicious version 0.23.3 was published to PyPI and Docker Hub, and removed after about 12 hours. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates a real-world supply-chain attack vector that can be studied for offensive simulations.)

The attack exploited a vulnerability in a GitHub Action: attackers posted malicious code to a pull request, which ran a bash script inside the developer's account to steal signing keys and tokens. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this technique can be used to compromise CI/CD pipelines in red team engagements.)

The malware scoured systems for user profiles, warehouse credentials, cloud provider keys, API tokens, SSH keys, and .env files. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: indicates high-value targets for credential theft in post-exploitation.)

Users are advised to check for a marker file at /tmp/.trinny-security-update (Linux/macOS) or %TEMP%\.trinny-security-update (Windows) to determine if the payload executed. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: useful IOC for detection and for creating similar persistence mechanisms.)

HD Moore noted that user-developed repository workflows (e.g., GitHub Actions) are notorious for vulnerabilities, and recommended using a package to check for such flaws. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: highlights a common weak point in open-source projects that can be targeted.)
methodology_steps: 1. Check installed version: run 'pip show elementary-data | grep Version' to see if it's 0.23.3.
2. If compromised, uninstall: 'pip uninstall elementary-data', then install safe version: 'pip install elementary-data==0.23.4'. Pin to 0.23.4 in requirements/lockfiles.
3. Delete cache files to remove any artifacts.
4. Check for marker file: on macOS/Linux look for /tmp/.trinny-security-update; on Windows look for %TEMP%\.trinny-security-update. If present, the payload executed.
5. Rotate all credentials accessible from the environment: dbt profiles, warehouse credentials, cloud provider keys, API tokens, SSH keys, .env file contents. Pay special attention to CI/CD runners.
6. Contact security team to hunt for unauthorized usage of exposed credentials using IOCs provided in the article.
conclusions: The element-data compromise is a textbook supply-chain attack: attackers exploited a GitHub Actions workflow vulnerability to steal signing keys and publish a malicious update. This demonstrates that even popular, well-maintained packages can be weaponized if CI/CD pipelines are not hardened.

The incident reinforces the need for developers to audit their GitHub Actions workflows for vulnerabilities that allow pull requests to execute arbitrary code. Tools like the one mentioned by HD Moore can help identify such flaws.

Users must treat any environment where the malicious package ran as fully compromised and rotate all credentials immediately. The marker file provides a simple detection mechanism, but absence does not guarantee safety.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests as it details a real-world supply-chain attack via CI/CD pipeline exploitation. You can study the attack vector (malicious pull request triggering a bash script in GitHub Actions) to simulate similar compromises in your own red team exercises. The IOCs (marker file, credential types targeted) provide practical indicators for detection and post-exploitation scenarios. Additionally, as a programmer, understanding how to secure GitHub Actions workflows is crucial for protecting your own projects and for advising clients. The article also offers actionable steps for incident response that you can incorporate into your security toolkit.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a honeypot environment that mimics a vulnerable GitHub Actions workflow to practice exploiting and defending against this attack vector. Also, integrate the marker file check into your post-exploitation enumeration scripts to quickly identify if a target has been previously compromised by this malware.
